skill.name: rust-development
skill.description: Rust development guidance for Guts projects using idiomatic code, structured errors, async patterns, testing, and Commonware primitives.
skill.summary: Apply practical Rust patterns for Guts services, libraries, tests, Cargo metadata, and Commonware integration.
skill.author: AbdelStark

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, sensitive-file, weak-crypto, reconnaissance, and heuristic issues. Review found these are Markdown examples, Rust identifiers, Cargo metadata, and legitimate development guidance, with no evidence of malicious intent or prompt injection.

low_findings.title: Development Command Examples
low_findings.description: The static command findings are from a fenced Bash example that recommends cargo fmt and cargo clippy before commits. These are normal Rust developer commands, but agents should still run them only in the user's intended repository.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible Markdown examples for formatting and linting. No dynamic input, shell metacharacter abuse, or hidden execution instruction was found.

low_findings.title: Repository URL in Cargo Metadata
low_findings.description: The network finding is a Cargo.toml repository field that points to the source project. It is metadata, not an instruction to send data or make an unauthorized network request.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL appears inside a Cargo.toml example as a repository value. There is no fetch, upload, webhook, credential transfer, or runtime network operation.

low_findings.title: False Positive Sensitive and Cryptography Matches
low_findings.description: The reported certificate/key and weak-cryptography findings map to Rust project text, Ed25519 signing examples, and Cargo description fields. Ed25519 is a modern signature algorithm, and no private key files or weak cryptographic algorithm usage were found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relevant lines contain prose and an Ed25519 API example. I did not find evidence of certificate files, hardcoded secrets, DES, MD5, SHA1, or similar weak crypto use.

low_findings.title: False Positive Reconnaissance and Heuristic Combination
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance and dangerous-combination findings are not supported by semantic context. The file contains Rust best practices, dependency examples, and module patterns, with no credential access or exfiltration flow.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious combination is explained by Markdown code examples plus a repository URL. No environment access, secret collection, command obfuscation, or data exfiltration was present.

content.value_statement: Rust teams need consistent patterns for safe async services, errors, tests, and package metadata. This skill gives focused guidance for Guts projects that use Commonware primitives.
actual_capabilities: Recommends idiomatic Rust structure for libraries, services, modules, and public exports.
Guides error handling with thiserror for libraries and anyhow for applications.
Shows async service patterns using Tokio channels and structured request replies.
Provides testing guidance for async Rust code using tokio test patterns.
Documents Commonware crate choices for cryptography, p2p, consensus, storage, and codec needs.
Suggests Cargo.toml metadata, lint settings, and dependency organization for workspace projects.
limitations: It is tailored to the Guts project and may need adaptation for other Rust codebases.
It provides guidance and examples, not an executable build or test harness.
It does not replace security review for cryptographic protocols or consensus logic.
It assumes familiarity with Rust, Cargo, Tokio, and workspace dependency management.

use_cases.title: Standardize a Rust Service
use_cases.description: Apply consistent module layout, error types, async request handling, tests, and lint rules before merging a service.

use_cases.title: Review Commonware Integration
use_cases.description: Check that a Guts component uses the intended Commonware crates for signing, networking, storage, serialization, and consensus.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Library for Release
use_cases.description: Improve public docs, Cargo metadata, workspace dependencies, lint configuration, and test coverage for a Rust library.

prompt_templates.title: Review Basic Rust Style
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a small Rust module
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Rust module for idiomatic style, public documentation, error handling, and tests. Use the rust-development skill guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Design Error Handling
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design error handling for this Rust library. Recommend thiserror variants, Result aliases, conversions, and user-facing messages.

prompt_templates.title: Improve Async Service Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this async Rust service. Focus on structured concurrency, channel usage, request replies, ownership, and testability.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Guts Commonware Usage
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review of decentralized infrastructure code
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this Guts component for Commonware integration, Cargo configuration, lint settings, async patterns, tests, and performance risks.

output_examples.input: Review my Rust service before I open a pull request.
output: A prioritized review of style, errors, async flow, tests, and documentation.
Specific suggestions for module boundaries and public API exports.
A short checklist of commands and tests to run locally.

output_examples.input: Help me add Commonware cryptography to a Guts component.
output: A recommended integration approach using the relevant Commonware crate.
Notes on ownership, signing boundaries, and verification responsibilities.
Warnings about areas that still require protocol-level security review.

output_examples.input: Make my Cargo.toml ready for a workspace crate.
output: A review of package metadata, workspace dependencies, dev dependencies, and lint settings.
Suggested corrections for missing repository, license, keywords, or documentation expectations.

best_practices: Run formatting, linting, and tests in the intended repository before applying generated changes.
Use explicit error types for libraries and keep public errors documented.
Keep async services testable by isolating channels, commands, and response paths.
anti_patterns: Do not add unsafe Rust unless a clear need and review plan exist.
Do not hide shared mutable state behind async tasks when channels model the workflow better.
Do not treat example cryptography code as a complete protocol design.

faq.question: What kind of Rust project is this skill for?
faq.answer: It is written for Guts projects that use Rust, Tokio, Cargo workspaces, and Commonware primitives.

faq.question: Can it write production Rust code?
faq.answer: It can guide and review production code, but generated changes still need normal tests and human review.

faq.question: Does it execute commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It contains example cargo commands. Any command execution depends on the host AI tool and user approval model.

faq.question: Does it include network behavior?
faq.answer: No runtime network behavior was found. The only URL is repository metadata in a Cargo.toml example.

faq.question: Is the cryptography guidance complete?
faq.answer: No. It names Commonware cryptography and Ed25519 examples, but protocol security still requires expert review.

faq.question: Can I use it outside the Guts project?
faq.answer: Yes, but project-specific Commonware and naming guidance may need adaptation.
